7.1.1.body:
# Essential Linux Commands for Developers

As a backend developer, Linux is your production environment. Most servers run Linux, so fluency with the command line is non-negotiable.

## Navigation & File Operations

```bash
# Navigate and explore
pwd                    # Print working directory
ls -lah                # List files with details, hidden files, human-readable sizes
cd /var/log            # Change directory
tree -L 2 src/         # Show directory structure, 2 levels deep

# File operations
cp -r src/ backup/     # Copy recursively
mv old.txt new.txt     # Move/rename
rm -rf build/          # Remove recursively (CAREFUL!)
mkdir -p a/b/c         # Create nested directories
```

## Viewing & Searching Files

```bash
# View files
cat config.yaml        # Print entire file
less /var/log/syslog   # Paginated viewing
head -n 20 app.log     # First 20 lines
tail -f app.log        # Follow log output in real-time

# Search
grep -rn "ERROR" /var/log/   # Recursive search with line numbers
find / -name "*.py" -mtime -7  # Python files modified in last 7 days
which python3          # Find executable location
```

## Pipes & Redirection

```bash
# Chain commands
cat access.log | grep "500" | wc -l        # Count 500 errors
ps aux | grep python | awk '{print $2}'    # Get PIDs of Python processes
echo "backup" >> notes.txt                  # Append to file
curl -s api.example.com 2>/dev/null        # Suppress stderr
```

## Common Mistakes
- Running `rm -rf /` or `rm -rf *` without double-checking the current directory
- Forgetting `-r` flag when copying directories
- Using `cat` for large files instead of `less` or `tail`

## Key Takeaways
- Master `grep`, `find`, `awk`, and `sed` -- they solve 90% of text-processing tasks
- Always use `tail -f` for live log monitoring
- Pipe (`|`) is your best friend for composing powerful one-liners
7.1.1.intuition: Think of Linux commands like kitchen utensils. Each tool does one thing well (a knife cuts, a peeler peels), but combining them in sequence lets you prepare any dish. Pipes are like an assembly line connecting each station.

exercises.title: Log Analysis One-Liner
exercises.description: Given a web server access log file, write a single command pipeline that extracts all unique IP addresses that received a 500 status code, sorts them by frequency, and shows the top 10.
exercises.hint: Combine grep, awk, sort, uniq -c, sort -rn, and head in a pipeline.

exercises.title: File Organization Script
exercises.description: Use find and xargs to locate all .log files older than 30 days in /var/log and compress them with gzip.
exercises.hint: Use find with -mtime +30 -name '*.log' and pipe to xargs gzip.

exercises.title: System Reconnaissance
exercises.description: Write a sequence of commands to determine: disk usage of the current directory, available memory, number of CPU cores, and the current Linux distribution.
exercises.hint: Use du -sh, free -h, nproc (or cat /proc/cpuinfo), and cat /etc/os-release.

problems.problem: Permission denied when running a script
problems.cause: The script file does not have execute permission.
problems.solution: Run `chmod +x script.sh` to add execute permission, then run with `./script.sh`.

problems.problem: Command not found after installing a tool
problems.cause: The binary is not in your PATH environment variable.
problems.solution: Find the binary with `which` or `find`, then add its directory to PATH in your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc: `export PATH=$PATH:/new/path`.

problems.problem: Accidentally deleted important files with rm
problems.cause: rm permanently removes files -- there is no trash/recycle bin by default.
problems.solution: Use `rm -i` for interactive confirmation. Consider aliasing rm to `rm -i` in your shell config. For critical systems, use a trash utility like `trash-cli`.

further_reading.title: The Linux Command Line (William Shotts)
further_reading.note: Free online book -- the definitive beginner-to-intermediate guide

further_reading.title: explainshell.com
further_reading.note: Paste any command to get a breakdown of every flag and argument

7.1.2.body:
# SSH, Permissions, and File System

Secure Shell (SSH) is how you access remote servers. Understanding file permissions and the Linux filesystem hierarchy keeps your deployments secure and organized.

## SSH Essentials

```bash
# Generate an SSH key pair
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "dev@example.com"
# Creates ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 (private) and ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub (public)

# Connect to a remote server
ssh user@192.168.1.100
ssh -i ~/.ssh/mykey.pem ubuntu@ec2-instance.amazonaws.com

# Copy files securely
scp app.tar.gz user@server:/opt/apps/
rsync -avz ./deploy/ user@server:/var/www/app/

# SSH config for convenience (~/.ssh/config)
# Host myserver
#   HostName 192.168.1.100
#   User deploy
#   IdentityFile ~/.ssh/deploy_key
```

## File Permissions

```bash
# Permission format: rwxrwxrwx (owner/group/others)
ls -la
# -rw-r--r-- 1 deploy www-data 4096 Jan 15 config.yaml

chmod 755 deploy.sh    # rwxr-xr-x (owner full, others read+execute)
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519  # rw------- (owner only -- REQUIRED for SSH keys)
chown deploy:www-data /var/www/app  # Change owner and group
```

## Linux Filesystem Hierarchy

```bash
/etc/          # Configuration files (nginx.conf, crontab)
/var/log/      # Application and system logs
/var/www/      # Web server document root
/opt/          # Optional/third-party software
/tmp/          # Temporary files (cleared on reboot)
/home/user/    # User home directories
/usr/local/bin # Locally installed binaries
```

## Common Mistakes
- Setting SSH private keys to world-readable (must be 600 or 400)
- Running everything as root instead of using a dedicated deploy user
- Forgetting to add your public key to `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the server

## Key Takeaways
- Always use SSH keys, never password auth on production servers
- Follow the principle of least privilege for file permissions
- Use `~/.ssh/config` to simplify connections to multiple servers
7.1.2.intuition: SSH is like having a secure private tunnel between your laptop and a remote building. The private key is your unique badge that never leaves your pocket, while the public key is a copy of your badge reader installed at the building entrance. Only your badge opens your reader.

exercises.title: SSH Key Setup
exercises.description: Generate an Ed25519 SSH key pair, add it to a remote server's authorized_keys, and verify passwordless login. Then create an SSH config entry for the server.
exercises.hint: Use ssh-keygen, ssh-copy-id, and edit ~/.ssh/config.

exercises.title: Permission Audit
exercises.description: Write a find command that locates all world-writable files (o+w) in /var/www/ and generates a report. Then fix the permissions to be owner-writable only.
exercises.hint: Use find /var/www -perm -o+w and chmod o-w.

problems.problem: SSH connection refused
problems.cause: SSH daemon is not running or is on a non-standard port, or a firewall is blocking port 22.
problems.solution: Check the SSH service status: `sudo systemctl status sshd`. Verify the port in /etc/ssh/sshd_config. Check firewall rules: `sudo ufw status` or `sudo iptables -L`.
symptoms: ssh: connect to host X port 22: Connection refused

problems.problem: Permissions too open error for SSH key
problems.cause: Your private key file has permissions that are too permissive.
problems.solution: Run `chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519` to restrict to owner-only read/write.

problems.problem: Cannot write to /var/www as deploy user
problems.cause: The directory is owned by root and the deploy user lacks write permission.
problems.solution: Change ownership: `sudo chown -R deploy:www-data /var/www/app` and set group-write: `chmod -R g+w /var/www/app`.

7.1.3.body:
# systemd, cron, and Process Management

In production, your Python applications run as background services. systemd manages services, cron schedules recurring tasks, and understanding process management prevents resource leaks.

## systemd Service Files

```bash
# /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service
[Unit]
Description=My Python Web App
After=network.target postgresql.service

[Service]
User=deploy
WorkingDirectory=/opt/myapp
ExecStart=/opt/myapp/venv/bin/gunicorn -w 4 -b 0.0.0.0:8000 app:create_app()
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
EnvironmentFile=/opt/myapp/.env

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

```bash
# Managing services
sudo systemctl start myapp      # Start the service
sudo systemctl enable myapp     # Start on boot
sudo systemctl status myapp     # Check status
sudo journalctl -u myapp -f     # Follow service logs
```

## Cron Jobs

```bash
# Edit crontab
crontab -e

# Format: minute hour day month weekday command
*/5 * * * *  /opt/myapp/venv/bin/python /opt/myapp/tasks/cleanup.py
0 2 * * *    /usr/bin/pg_dump mydb > /backups/db_$(date +\%F).sql
0 */6 * * *  /opt/myapp/venv/bin/python /opt/myapp/tasks/sync_data.py >> /var/log/sync.log 2>&1
```

## Process Management

```bash
ps aux | grep python            # List Python processes
top -p $(pgrep -d, gunicorn)    # Monitor specific processes
kill -SIGTERM 12345             # Graceful shutdown
kill -SIGKILL 12345             # Force kill (last resort)
nohup python long_task.py &     # Run in background, survive logout
```

## Common Mistakes
- Forgetting `Restart=always` in service files, causing downtime after crashes
- Not redirecting cron output, losing error messages silently
- Using SIGKILL instead of SIGTERM, preventing graceful cleanup

## Key Takeaways
- Use systemd for long-running services, cron for scheduled tasks
- Always check `journalctl` for service troubleshooting
- Prefer SIGTERM over SIGKILL to allow graceful shutdown
7.1.3.intuition: systemd is like a building manager who ensures all the tenants (services) are in their offices when the building opens and restarts anyone who crashes. Cron is like a timer that rings at set intervals to remind you to do recurring chores -- take out the trash every night, check the mailbox every hour.

exercises.title: Create a systemd Service
exercises.description: Write a systemd service file for a FastAPI application that uses Uvicorn, restarts on failure, starts after the network is available, and loads environment variables from a .env file.
exercises.hint: Use ExecStart with uvicorn, set Restart=on-failure, After=network.target, and EnvironmentFile.

exercises.title: Scheduled Database Backup
exercises.description: Set up a cron job that runs pg_dump every night at 2 AM, compresses the output with gzip, names the file with the current date, and keeps only the last 7 backups.
exercises.hint: Combine pg_dump, gzip, date formatting, and find -mtime +7 -delete in a small shell script called by cron.

exercises.title: Process Monitoring Script
exercises.description: Write a bash script that checks if a gunicorn process is running. If not, restart the systemd service and send an alert to a log file with a timestamp.
exercises.hint: Use pgrep gunicorn, check the exit code, and use systemctl restart if needed.

problems.problem: systemd service fails to start
problems.cause: Incorrect ExecStart path, missing dependencies, or permission issues.
problems.solution: Check logs with `sudo journalctl -u myapp -n 50 --no-pager`. Verify the ExecStart binary path exists and is executable. Ensure the User has permissions to the WorkingDirectory.

problems.problem: Cron job runs but produces no output or effect
problems.cause: Cron runs with a minimal environment -- PATH and other variables differ from your shell.
problems.solution: Use absolute paths for all commands in cron. Redirect output: `>> /var/log/myjob.log 2>&1`. Set PATH at the top of the crontab.
symptoms: Script works manually but not via cron

further_reading.title: Crontab Guru - Cron Expression Editor
further_reading.note: Interactive tool to build and verify cron schedule expressions

7.1.4.body:
# Shell Scripting Basics for Backend Devs

Shell scripts automate repetitive tasks: deployments, database migrations, health checks, and environment setup.

## Script Fundamentals

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail  # Exit on error, undefined vars, pipe failures

APP_NAME="myapp"
DEPLOY_DIR="/opt/${APP_NAME}"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
echo "Deploying ${APP_NAME} at ${TIMESTAMP}"
```

## Conditionals and Loops

```bash
if command -v docker &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Docker is installed"
else
    echo "Docker not found. Installing..."
    curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh
fi

SERVERS=("web1" "web2" "web3")
for server in "${SERVERS[@]}"; do
    ssh deploy@"${server}" "cd /opt/app && git pull && sudo systemctl restart app"
done
```

## A Simple Deploy Script

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
APP_DIR="/opt/myapp"

[ -d "$APP_DIR" ] || { echo "ERROR: App dir not found" >&2; exit 1; }
tar -czf "/opt/backups/backup_$(date +%F).tar.gz" "$APP_DIR"

cd "$APP_DIR"
git pull origin main
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
sudo systemctl restart myapp
echo "Deployment complete!"
```

## Functions and Error Handling

```bash
log() { echo "[$(date '+%H:%M:%S')] $*"; }
die() { echo "ERROR: $*" >&2; exit 1; }

check_health() {
    local status
    status=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' "$1")
    [ "$status" -eq 200 ] || die "Health check failed: HTTP $status"
}
```

## Common Mistakes
- Forgetting `set -euo pipefail`, causing scripts to silently continue after errors
- Not quoting variables: `$VAR` vs `"$VAR"` -- unquoted vars break on spaces

## Key Takeaways
- Always start scripts with `set -euo pipefail`
- Quote all variable expansions to handle spaces in paths
- Use functions to keep scripts organized and reusable
7.1.4.intuition: A shell script is like a recipe card for your kitchen (server). Instead of remembering every step each time you cook (deploy), you write it down once. `set -euo pipefail` is your safety checklist -- stop immediately if any step goes wrong rather than serving a half-cooked dish.

exercises.title: Environment Setup Script
exercises.description: Write a bash script that sets up a Python development environment: checks for Python 3.10+, creates a virtual environment, installs dependencies from requirements.txt, copies .env.example to .env if .env doesn't exist, and runs migrations.
exercises.hint: Use python3 --version, compare versions, and check file existence with [ -f .env ].

exercises.title: Health Check & Alerting Script
exercises.description: Write a script that pings a list of URLs, checks for HTTP 200, and logs failures with timestamps. Make it suitable for running via cron every minute.
exercises.hint: Use curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' and loop through an array of URLs.

exercises.title: Rolling Deployment Script
exercises.description: Write a deploy script that deploys to 3 servers one at a time, runs a health check after each, and rolls back if any server fails.
exercises.hint: Use a for loop, ssh for remote commands, a health check function, and trap for cleanup.

problems.problem: Script works interactively but fails in cron or CI
problems.cause: The script depends on environment variables or PATH entries that exist in an interactive shell but not in cron/CI environments.
problems.solution: Use absolute paths for all commands. Source required env files explicitly. Add `export PATH=/usr/local/bin:$PATH` at the top.
symptoms: command not found errors in cron logs
Scripts pass locally but fail in GitHub Actions

problems.problem: Script breaks on filenames with spaces
problems.cause: Unquoted variable expansions undergo word splitting.
problems.solution: Always double-quote variables: use `"$file"` not `$file`. Use `"${array[@]}"` for arrays.
problems.code:
# Wrong
for f in $files; do
# Right
for f in "${files[@]}"; do

further_reading.title: ShellCheck - Shell Script Linter
further_reading.note: Paste scripts to find bugs and get suggestions -- essential for bash

7.2.1.body:
# Dockerfile: Building Python Application Images

Docker packages your application and all its dependencies into a portable container image. A Dockerfile is the recipe for building that image.

## Basic Python Dockerfile

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    gcc libpq-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies (cached layer)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Create non-root user
RUN adduser --disabled-password --gecos '' appuser
USER appuser

# Expose port and set entrypoint
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "-w", "4", "-b", "0.0.0.0:8000", "app:create_app()"]
```

## Building and Running

```bash
# Build the image
docker build -t myapp:latest .

# Run the container
docker run -d -p 8000:8000 --name myapp \
  -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://... \
  myapp:latest

# View logs
docker logs -f myapp
```

## .dockerignore

```
__pycache__
*.pyc
.git
.env
venv/
.pytest_cache
node_modules
```

## Common Mistakes
- Copying code before installing dependencies, busting the pip install cache on every code change
- Running containers as root -- always create and switch to a non-root user
- Not using `.dockerignore`, resulting in bloated images with .git and __pycache__

## Key Takeaways
- Order Dockerfile instructions from least to most frequently changing for optimal caching
- Use `python:3.x-slim` instead of the full image to reduce size by ~600MB
- Always set `PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1` so logs appear immediately in `docker logs`
7.2.1.intuition: A Dockerfile is like flat-pack furniture instructions. Each instruction (FROM, RUN, COPY) is a step, and Docker caches each step as a layer. If step 5 changes but steps 1-4 haven't, Docker reuses the cached layers and only rebuilds from step 5 onward -- like skipping furniture steps you've already completed.

exercises.title: Build a FastAPI Docker Image
exercises.description: Write a Dockerfile for a FastAPI application that uses Uvicorn, installs from a pyproject.toml, creates a non-root user, and exposes port 8000. Build and verify it runs correctly.
exercises.hint: Use pip install . if using pyproject.toml. Use CMD ["uvicorn", "app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0"].

exercises.title: Layer Caching Optimization
exercises.description: Given a Dockerfile that copies all code first then installs dependencies, refactor it to maximize layer caching. Measure the build time difference.
exercises.hint: Copy requirements.txt and pip install BEFORE copying the rest of the application code.

problems.problem: Docker build fails with 'pip install' errors
problems.cause: Missing system-level build dependencies (gcc, libffi-dev, etc.) needed to compile Python packages.
problems.solution: Add a RUN step before pip install to install build dependencies: `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y gcc libffi-dev`.

problems.problem: Container runs but logs don't appear
problems.cause: Python buffers stdout by default inside containers.
problems.solution: Set `ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1` in the Dockerfile or pass `-e PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1` at runtime.
symptoms: Logs only appear when container stops

problems.problem: Image size is over 1GB
problems.cause: Using the full python base image and not cleaning up apt caches.
problems.solution: Use `python:3.12-slim` as the base, add `--no-install-recommends` to apt-get, and `rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` after installing. Use multi-stage builds for even smaller images.
problems.code: docker images myapp  # Check image size

further_reading.title: Docker for Python Developers
further_reading.note: Practical tips specific to Python applications

7.2.2.body:
# Docker Compose: Multi-Container Applications

Real applications need multiple services: a web app, database, cache, worker. Docker Compose defines and runs multi-container setups from a single YAML file.

## docker-compose.yml

```yaml
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/mydb
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    volumes:
      - .:/app
    restart: unless-stopped

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: pass
      POSTGRES_DB: mydb
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U user -d mydb"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine

  worker:
    build: .
    command: celery -A tasks worker --loglevel=info
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

## Essential Commands

```bash
docker compose up -d              # Start all services in background
docker compose logs -f web        # Follow logs for web service
docker compose exec web bash      # Shell into running container
docker compose down -v            # Stop and remove containers + volumes
docker compose ps                 # List running services
```

## Common Mistakes
- Using `depends_on` without health checks -- the DB container starts but Postgres isn't ready
- Hardcoding passwords in docker-compose.yml committed to git
- Mounting volumes in production (use only for development)

## Key Takeaways
- Use health checks with `depends_on` conditions for reliable startup order
- Named volumes persist data across container restarts
- Services communicate via service names as hostnames (e.g., `db`, `redis`)
7.2.2.intuition: Docker Compose is like a stage director's script. Each service (actor) has its entrance cue (depends_on), costume (image/build), and position (ports). The director (Compose) ensures everyone appears on stage in the right order and can talk to each other by name.

exercises.title: Full-Stack Compose Setup
exercises.description: Create a docker-compose.yml for a FastAPI app with PostgreSQL, Redis, and a Celery worker. Include health checks for Postgres, proper depends_on ordering, and a named volume for database persistence.
exercises.hint: Use the pg_isready command for Postgres healthcheck and service names for inter-container networking.

exercises.title: Development vs Production Compose
exercises.description: Create a base docker-compose.yml and a docker-compose.override.yml for development (with volume mounts and debug mode) and a docker-compose.prod.yml for production (without volume mounts, with restart policies).
exercises.hint: Use `docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up` for production.

exercises.title: Compose with Environment Files
exercises.description: Refactor a docker-compose.yml to use an .env file for all secrets and configuration values. Ensure the .env file is in .gitignore and create a .env.example template.
exercises.hint: Use env_file directive or ${VARIABLE} syntax in docker-compose.yml.

problems.problem: Web container crashes because database isn't ready
problems.cause: depends_on only waits for the container to start, not for the service inside to be ready.
problems.solution: Add a healthcheck to the database service and use `depends_on: db: condition: service_healthy`.
symptoms: FATAL: the database system is starting up

problems.problem: Data lost when running docker compose down
problems.cause: Using `docker compose down -v` removes named volumes containing database data.
problems.solution: Use `docker compose down` without `-v` to preserve volumes. Only use `-v` when you intentionally want to reset data.

problems.problem: Cannot connect to service from host machine
problems.cause: Port mapping is missing or the service is binding to 127.0.0.1 inside the container.
problems.solution: Ensure the service binds to `0.0.0.0` inside the container and that `ports` mapping is defined in docker-compose.yml.
symptoms: curl: (7) Failed to connect to localhost port 8000

further_reading.title: Awesome Compose - Sample Apps
further_reading.note: Official collection of Docker Compose examples for various stacks

7.2.3.body:
# Multi-Stage Builds and Image Optimization

Production Docker images should be small, secure, and fast to deploy. Multi-stage builds separate build-time dependencies from the runtime image.

## Multi-Stage Build

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends gcc libpq-dev
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

# Stage 2: Runtime
FROM python:3.12-slim AS runtime
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends libpq5 \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
COPY . .
RUN adduser --disabled-password appuser
USER appuser
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "-w", "4", "-b", "0.0.0.0:8000", "app:create_app()"]
```

## Optimization Techniques

```bash
# Compare image sizes
# python:3.12        ~1.0GB
# python:3.12-slim   ~150MB
# python:3.12-alpine ~50MB (compatibility issues with some packages)

# Analyze layers
docker history myapp:latest
```

```dockerfile
# Combine RUN commands to reduce layers
RUN apt-get update \
    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends gcc \
    && pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt \
    && apt-get purge -y gcc && apt-get autoremove -y \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Pin base image versions
FROM python:3.12.1-slim
```

## Common Mistakes
- Including build tools (gcc) in the final image
- Using Alpine for Python -- musl libc causes compatibility issues
- Not pinning base image versions

## Key Takeaways
- Multi-stage builds can reduce image sizes by 50-80%
- Prefer `slim` over `alpine` for Python images
- Use `--no-cache-dir` with pip to avoid storing caches in the image
7.2.3.intuition: Multi-stage builds are like building furniture in a workshop (stage 1) and then moving only the finished piece to your apartment (stage 2). You don't bring the table saw, wood shavings, and screwdriver home -- you only bring the chair. The build tools stay behind, resulting in a cleaner, smaller home.

exercises.title: Optimize an Image
exercises.description: Take a basic Dockerfile that uses python:3.12 (full image), installs gcc and dev libraries, and produces a 1.2GB image. Refactor it with multi-stage builds to get it under 200MB.
exercises.hint: Use a builder stage for compilation, copy only /install to a slim runtime stage, and remove apt caches.

exercises.title: Layer Analysis
exercises.description: Build an image, use `docker history` to identify the largest layers, and refactor the Dockerfile to minimize total size. Document the before and after sizes.
exercises.hint: Combine RUN commands, clean up in the same layer, and use --no-cache-dir.

problems.problem: Multi-stage build copies packages but they fail at runtime
problems.cause: Build-time packages were compiled against libraries that aren't in the runtime stage.
problems.solution: Ensure runtime system libraries (like libpq5 for psycopg2) are installed in the final stage. Only build-time headers (libpq-dev) can be excluded.
symptoms: ImportError: libpq.so.5: cannot open shared object file

problems.problem: Docker build takes too long in CI
problems.cause: Layer cache is not being reused between CI runs.
problems.solution: Use Docker BuildKit cache mounts or push/pull cache images: `docker build --cache-from myapp:latest`.
symptoms: Every CI build reinstalls all pip packages from scratch

further_reading.title: Dive - Docker Image Explorer
further_reading.note: Interactive tool to analyze Docker image layers and find wasted space

7.2.4.body:
# Docker Networking and Volumes

Containers need to communicate with each other and persist data. Docker networking and volumes handle these two critical concerns.

## Docker Networking

```bash
# Create a custom network
docker network create app-network

# Containers on the same network reach each other by name
docker run -d --name db --network app-network postgres:16
docker run -d --name web --network app-network -p 8000:8000 myapp
```

```yaml
# Compose: isolate with multiple networks
services:
  web:
    networks: [frontend, backend]
  db:
    networks: [backend]  # Not accessible from frontend
  nginx:
    networks: [frontend]

networks:
  frontend:
  backend:
```

## Docker Volumes

```bash
# Named volumes (managed by Docker)
docker volume create pgdata
docker run -d -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:16

# Bind mounts (map host directory, for development)
docker run -d -v $(pwd)/src:/app/src myapp
```

```yaml
services:
  db:
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data     # Named volume
      - ./init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql  # Bind mount
volumes:
  pgdata:
```

## Network Types

| Type | Use Case |
|------|----------|
| bridge | Default. Containers on same host |
| host | Container shares host network |
| overlay | Multi-host (Docker Swarm) |

## Common Mistakes
- Using the default bridge network (no DNS resolution by name)
- Using bind mounts for database storage in production
- Forgetting that container filesystems are ephemeral

## Key Takeaways
- Use custom bridge networks so containers resolve each other by name
- Use named volumes for persistent data, bind mounts for development
- Isolate services using multiple networks
7.2.4.intuition: Docker networking is like rooms in an office building. Services on the same network are in the same room and can talk directly. Different networks are different rooms with locked doors between them. Volumes are like filing cabinets -- even if you demolish and rebuild the room (container), the cabinet (volume) keeps your files safe.

exercises.title: Network Isolation
exercises.description: Create a Docker Compose setup with three services: nginx (frontend), a Python API (backend), and PostgreSQL (database). Configure networks so nginx can reach the API, the API can reach the database, but nginx cannot directly reach the database.
exercises.hint: Create two networks: 'frontend' and 'backend'. Put nginx and API on frontend, API and database on backend.

exercises.title: Volume Backup and Restore
exercises.description: Write a bash script that backs up a named Docker volume to a tar.gz file and can restore it. Test with a PostgreSQL database volume.
exercises.hint: Use a temporary container: docker run --rm -v pgdata:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar czf /backup/pgdata.tar.gz /data

problems.problem: Containers can't communicate by service name
problems.cause: Using the default bridge network which doesn't provide DNS resolution between containers.
problems.solution: Create a custom network with `docker network create mynet` and attach containers to it, or use Docker Compose which creates a custom network automatically.

problems.problem: Database data lost after docker compose down
problems.cause: Running `docker compose down -v` which deletes named volumes, or not using a named volume at all.
problems.solution: Define a named volume in the `volumes:` top-level key and mount it to the database data directory. Only use `-v` flag with `down` when you intentionally want to reset.

problems.problem: Bind mount shows empty directory inside container
problems.cause: The host path doesn't exist or is incorrect. On macOS/Windows, the path may not be shared with Docker.
problems.solution: Verify the host path exists. On Docker Desktop, check File Sharing settings to ensure the directory is in a shared path.

7.3.1.body:
# GitHub Actions: Workflows, Jobs, and Steps

GitHub Actions automates your build, test, and deployment pipeline. Every push, PR, or schedule can trigger workflows.

## Workflow Structure

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI Pipeline
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ['3.11', '3.12']
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: testpass
          POSTGRES_DB: testdb
        ports: ['5432:5432']
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/pip
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-pip-${{ hashFiles('requirements.txt') }}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest --tb=short -q
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:testpass@localhost:5432/testdb
```

## Key Concepts

- **Workflow**: YAML file in `.github/workflows/` triggered by events
- **Job**: Steps running on the same runner; jobs run in parallel by default
- **Step**: Shell command (`run:`) or reusable action (`uses:`)
- **Matrix**: Run the same job across multiple configurations

## Common Mistakes
- Not caching dependencies, causing slow CI builds
- Hardcoding secrets instead of using `${{ secrets.MY_SECRET }}`

## Key Takeaways
- Use `services` to spin up databases for integration tests
- Cache pip dependencies with `actions/cache` to cut build times
- Use matrix strategy to test across multiple Python versions
7.3.1.intuition: GitHub Actions is like a factory assembly line that starts automatically when you push code. Each job is a separate workstation, and each step is a specific task at that station. The matrix strategy is like running parallel assembly lines -- one for each configuration you need to test.

exercises.title: Build a CI Workflow
exercises.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that runs on PRs to main, sets up Python 3.12, installs dependencies, runs linting with ruff, type checking with mypy, and tests with pytest. Include pip caching.
exercises.hint: Use actions/checkout@v4, actions/setup-python@v5, actions/cache@v4, and separate run steps for each tool.

exercises.title: Matrix Testing with Services
exercises.description: Extend the CI workflow to test against Python 3.11 and 3.12, with both PostgreSQL 15 and 16. Use the matrix strategy to cover all combinations.
exercises.hint: Use strategy.matrix with python-version and postgres-version arrays, and reference them in the services section.

exercises.title: Reusable Workflow
exercises.description: Extract the test job into a reusable workflow that can be called from other repositories. Accept Python version and test command as inputs.
exercises.hint: Use workflow_call trigger with inputs and secrets.

problems.problem: Service container isn't ready when tests start
problems.cause: The service container has started but the service inside (e.g., PostgreSQL) isn't ready to accept connections yet.
problems.solution: Add health check options to the service definition: `--health-cmd pg_isready --health-interval 10s --health-retries 5`.
symptoms: psycopg2.OperationalError: could not connect to server

problems.problem: Cache not being restored
problems.cause: The cache key doesn't match any existing cache. Often caused by changing the key format or requirements file.
problems.solution: Use `hashFiles('requirements.txt')` in the cache key. Add a restore-keys fallback: `restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-pip-`.
symptoms: Cache not found for input keys
pip install takes 2+ minutes every run

further_reading.title: Awesome GitHub Actions
further_reading.note: Curated list of useful GitHub Actions

7.3.2.body:
# Testing Pipelines: Lint, Type-Check, Test, Coverage

A robust CI pipeline enforces code quality through linting, type checking, test coverage thresholds, and security scanning.

## Complete Testing Pipeline

```yaml
name: Code Quality
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install ruff
      - run: ruff check .
      - run: ruff format --check .

  type-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt mypy
      - run: mypy src/ --strict

  test:
    needs: [lint, type-check]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt pytest pytest-cov
      - run: pytest --cov=src --cov-report=xml --cov-fail-under=80
```

## Local Configuration

```python
# pyproject.toml
[tool.ruff]
target-version = "py312"
line-length = 88

[tool.mypy]
strict = true

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
addopts = "--cov=src --cov-report=term-missing"

[tool.coverage.report]
fail_under = 80
exclude_lines = ["pragma: no cover", "if TYPE_CHECKING:"]
```

## Common Mistakes
- Not running lint before tests -- wasting CI time on broken code
- Setting coverage threshold too high initially (start at 70-80%)

## Key Takeaways
- Run fast checks (lint) first, slow checks (tests) after
- Use `needs:` to create job dependencies and fail fast
- Configure tools in pyproject.toml so local and CI behavior match
7.3.2.intuition: Think of a quality pipeline like airport security. First, a quick visual check (linting) catches obvious issues. Then a scanner (type checking) catches hidden problems. Finally, thorough inspection (tests with coverage) verifies everything works. Each stage is faster to fail than the next, saving everyone time.

exercises.title: Build a Quality Gate
exercises.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow with separate jobs for ruff linting, mypy type checking, pytest with coverage, and a security scan using pip-audit. Configure job dependencies so tests only run if lint and type-check pass.
exercises.hint: Use the needs keyword to create dependencies. Install pip-audit for dependency vulnerability scanning.

exercises.title: Pre-commit Hooks
exercises.description: Set up pre-commit hooks that run ruff, mypy, and pytest on staged files before each commit. Create a .pre-commit-config.yaml and integrate it with the CI pipeline.
exercises.hint: Use the pre-commit framework with ruff-pre-commit and local hooks for mypy.

problems.problem: mypy fails in CI but passes locally
problems.cause: Missing type stubs in CI, different mypy versions, or missing dependencies that provide type information.
problems.solution: Pin the mypy version in requirements. Install all dependencies including type stubs (types-requests, etc.) in CI. Use the same mypy configuration (pyproject.toml) everywhere.
symptoms: error: Library stubs not installed for 'requests'

problems.problem: Coverage percentage keeps dropping
problems.cause: New code is being added without corresponding tests.
problems.solution: Set `--cov-fail-under=80` in pytest to enforce a minimum threshold. Add coverage reporting to PRs so reviewers can see coverage changes. Use `# pragma: no cover` sparingly for untestable code.

7.3.3.body:
# Automated Deployment Strategies

CI gets your code tested; CD gets it to production safely. Different strategies trade off between speed, risk, and complexity.

## Deployment Strategies

**Blue-Green**: Two identical environments. Deploy to the inactive one, test it, switch traffic, keep the old one for rollback.

**Rolling Update**: Update instances one at a time, keeping the service available throughout.

**Canary**: Route a small percentage of traffic to the new version first.

## GitHub Actions CD Pipeline

```yaml
name: Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/ci.yml

  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Deploy to server
        env:
          SSH_KEY: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_SSH_KEY }}
        run: |
          echo "$SSH_KEY" > key.pem && chmod 600 key.pem
          ssh -i key.pem deploy@server 'cd /opt/app && \
            git pull origin main && \
            docker compose up -d --build && \
            docker compose exec web python manage.py migrate'
      - name: Health check
        run: |
          sleep 10
          curl -f https://myapp.com/health || exit 1
      - name: Notify on failure
        if: failure()
        run: curl -X POST ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }} -d '{"text":"Deploy failed!"}'
```

## Common Mistakes
- Deploying directly to production without a staging environment
- No rollback plan -- always keep the previous version ready
- Not running database migrations before switching traffic

## Key Takeaways
- Start with blue-green and evolve to canary as needed
- Always include health checks and automatic rollback
- Use GitHub Environments with required reviewers for production
7.3.3.intuition: Deployment strategies are like updating a restaurant's menu. Blue-green is having two kitchens -- cook the new menu in kitchen B while kitchen A serves customers, then swap. Rolling update is changing one dish at a time. Canary is offering the new dish to 10% of tables first to see if they like it before rolling it out to everyone.

exercises.title: Build a CD Pipeline
exercises.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that deploys to a staging server on pushes to develop and to production on pushes to main. Include health checks and Slack notifications for failures.
exercises.hint: Use the environment key with protection rules. Use if conditions to select the target.

exercises.title: Implement Blue-Green with Docker Compose
exercises.description: Write a deployment script that runs two Docker Compose stacks (blue and green), deploys to the inactive one, runs smoke tests, and switches nginx upstream to the new stack.
exercises.hint: Use two compose files with different port mappings and an nginx config that you reload with the new upstream.

exercises.title: Rollback Script
exercises.description: Create a rollback mechanism that keeps the last 3 deployed versions as tagged Docker images and can instantly rollback by switching the running container to a previous tag.
exercises.hint: Tag images with git SHA or timestamps. Use docker tag and docker compose up with the previous tag.

problems.problem: Deployment succeeds but application is broken
problems.cause: No post-deployment health checks or smoke tests.
problems.solution: Add a health check step after deployment: `curl -f https://myapp.com/health || rollback`. Implement a `/health` endpoint that verifies database connectivity and critical dependencies.
symptoms: No alerts triggered despite broken app

problems.problem: Database migration breaks rolling deployment
problems.cause: The new code requires a schema change that is incompatible with the old code still running.
problems.solution: Use backward-compatible migrations: add new columns as nullable, deploy code that handles both schemas, then clean up. Never rename or remove columns in the same deploy.
symptoms: Some requests fail with column not found

7.4.1.body:
# AWS Fundamentals: EC2, RDS, S3

AWS is the most widely used cloud platform. Three core services handle compute (EC2), databases (RDS), and storage (S3) -- the building blocks of most backend deployments.

## EC2 - Virtual Servers

```bash
# Launch and connect to an EC2 instance
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --instance-type t3.micro \
  --key-name my-key \
  --security-groups my-sg

ssh -i my-key.pem ubuntu@<public-ip>
```

```python
# Using boto3 to manage EC2
import boto3

ec2 = boto3.client('ec2', region_name='us-east-1')

# List running instances
response = ec2.describe_instances(
    Filters=[{'Name': 'instance-state-name', 'Values': ['running']}]
)
for reservation in response['Reservations']:
    for instance in reservation['Instances']:
        print(f"{instance['InstanceId']}: {instance['PublicIpAddress']}")
```

## RDS - Managed Databases

```bash
# Create a PostgreSQL RDS instance
aws rds create-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier myapp-db \
  --db-instance-class db.t3.micro \
  --engine postgres \
  --master-username admin \
  --master-user-password secretpass \
  --allocated-storage 20
```

## S3 - Object Storage

```python
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

# Upload a file
s3.upload_file('report.pdf', 'my-bucket', 'reports/2024/report.pdf')

# Generate a pre-signed URL (temporary access)
url = s3.generate_presigned_url(
    'get_object',
    Params={'Bucket': 'my-bucket', 'Key': 'reports/2024/report.pdf'},
    ExpiresIn=3600  # 1 hour
)
```

## Common Mistakes
- Leaving EC2 security groups wide open (0.0.0.0/0 on all ports)
- Making RDS publicly accessible -- always keep it in a private subnet
- Storing AWS credentials in code instead of using IAM roles or environment variables

## Key Takeaways
- Use IAM roles for EC2 instances instead of hardcoded credentials
- RDS handles backups, patching, and failover -- don't run databases on raw EC2
- Use S3 pre-signed URLs for temporary, secure file access
7.4.1.intuition: Think of AWS like renting office space. EC2 is renting a desk with a computer (you manage the software). RDS is hiring an IT team to manage your database server (they handle backups and updates). S3 is an infinitely expandable filing cabinet where you pay only for the drawers you use.

exercises.title: Deploy a Python App to EC2
exercises.description: Launch a t3.micro EC2 instance, SSH in, install Python and your application, set up a systemd service, and configure a security group to allow HTTP traffic on port 80.
exercises.hint: Use user data scripts for automated setup. Open port 80 in the security group and set up nginx to proxy to your app.

exercises.title: S3 File Upload API
exercises.description: Build a FastAPI endpoint that accepts file uploads, stores them in S3 with a unique key, and returns a pre-signed download URL valid for 1 hour.
exercises.hint: Use boto3's upload_fileobj for streaming uploads and generate_presigned_url for download links.

exercises.title: RDS Connection from Python
exercises.description: Create an RDS PostgreSQL instance, configure security groups to allow access from your EC2 instance, and write a Python script using SQLAlchemy to connect and run queries.
exercises.hint: Put EC2 and RDS in the same VPC. Use the RDS endpoint as the database host in your connection string.

problems.problem: Cannot connect to RDS from EC2
problems.cause: Security group on RDS doesn't allow inbound connections from the EC2 instance's security group.
problems.solution: Edit the RDS security group to allow inbound PostgreSQL (port 5432) from the EC2 instance's security group ID.
symptoms: Connection timed out to RDS endpoint

problems.problem: S3 access denied despite correct credentials
problems.cause: The IAM policy doesn't grant the required S3 actions, or the bucket policy denies access.
problems.solution: Check the IAM policy attached to the user/role. Ensure it includes `s3:PutObject`, `s3:GetObject`, etc. for the specific bucket ARN.

further_reading.title: AWS Free Tier
further_reading.note: Learn without cost -- 750 hours of t2.micro EC2, 20GB RDS, 5GB S3 free per month for 12 months

7.4.2.body:
# AWS Lambda and Serverless Python

AWS Lambda runs your code without managing servers. You pay only for compute time consumed -- no traffic, no cost.

## Lambda Function Basics

```python
import json

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    """Handler receives event data and runtime context."""
    name = event.get('queryStringParameters', {}).get('name', 'World')
    return {
        'statusCode': 200,
        'headers': {'Content-Type': 'application/json'},
        'body': json.dumps({'message': f'Hello, {name}!'})
    }
```

## Deploying with AWS CLI

```bash
zip function.zip lambda_function.py

aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name hello-api \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --handler lambda_function.lambda_handler \
  --zip-file fileb://function.zip \
  --role arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/lambda-role
```

## Lambda with Dependencies

```bash
# Install deps into a package directory
mkdir package
pip install -r requirements.txt -t package/
cd package && zip -r ../deploy.zip . && cd ..
zip deploy.zip lambda_function.py
```

## API Gateway Integration

```python
def lambda_handler(event, context):
    method = event['httpMethod']
    path = event['path']
    body = json.loads(event.get('body') or '{}')
    params = event.get('pathParameters') or {}

    if method == 'GET' and path.startswith('/users'):
        user_id = params.get('id')
        return {'statusCode': 200, 'body': json.dumps(user)}
```

## Common Mistakes
- Putting heavy initialization inside the handler instead of outside (cold start optimization)
- Not setting memory/timeout limits -- default 3s timeout may be too short
- Including unnecessary files, hitting the 50MB package limit

## Key Takeaways
- Initialize clients outside the handler to reuse across invocations
- Use Lambda Layers for shared dependencies
- Lambda is best for event-driven tasks; for always-on APIs, consider ECS
7.4.2.intuition: Lambda is like a vending machine versus a restaurant. A restaurant (EC2) is always open, paying rent and staff even when empty. A vending machine (Lambda) sits idle at zero cost and only activates when someone inserts a coin. Great for sporadic use, but for a packed dinner rush, the restaurant is more efficient.

exercises.title: Build a Serverless API
exercises.description: Create a Lambda function that handles CRUD operations for a to-do list stored in DynamoDB. Connect it to API Gateway for HTTP access.
exercises.hint: Use boto3.resource('dynamodb') initialized outside the handler. Map HTTP methods to DynamoDB operations.

exercises.title: S3 Event Processor
exercises.description: Create a Lambda function triggered by S3 uploads that processes uploaded CSV files, transforms the data, and stores results in another S3 bucket.
exercises.hint: Configure S3 event notification to trigger Lambda. Use the event object to get bucket and key.

problems.problem: Lambda function times out
problems.cause: Default timeout is 3 seconds. Operations like database queries or API calls may take longer.
problems.solution: Increase the timeout (max 15 minutes): `aws lambda update-function-configuration --timeout 30`. For long tasks, consider Step Functions or SQS.

problems.problem: Import error for third-party packages
problems.cause: Dependencies are not included in the deployment package.
problems.solution: Install packages into the deployment zip: `pip install -r requirements.txt -t package/`. Or use Lambda Layers for shared dependencies.
symptoms: Unable to import module 'lambda_function': No module named 'requests'

problems.problem: Cold start latency is too high
problems.cause: Lambda loads your code and initializes the runtime on the first invocation after idle time.
problems.solution: Use Provisioned Concurrency to keep instances warm. Minimize package size. Move initialization (imports, DB connections) outside the handler function.

further_reading.title: Serverless Python with AWS Lambda - RealPython
AWS SAM (Serverless Application Model)
further_reading.note: Framework for building and deploying serverless applications locally and in AWS

7.4.3.body:
# GCP and Heroku: Alternative Platforms

AWS isn't the only option. GCP offers strong data/ML services, while Heroku provides the simplest path from code to production.

## Heroku - Simplicity First

```bash
heroku create my-python-app
git push heroku main
heroku addons:create heroku-postgresql:essential-0
heroku config:set SECRET_KEY=mysecret
heroku logs --tail
```

```python
# Procfile
# web: gunicorn app:create_app()
# worker: celery -A tasks worker
# release: python manage.py migrate
```

## GCP Cloud Run - Serverless Containers

```bash
gcloud builds submit --tag gcr.io/my-project/myapp
gcloud run deploy myapp \
  --image gcr.io/my-project/myapp \
  --platform managed \
  --allow-unauthenticated
```

## GCP Cloud Functions

```python
import functions_framework

@functions_framework.http
def hello(request):
    name = request.args.get('name', 'World')
    return f'Hello, {name}!'
```

```bash
gcloud functions deploy hello --runtime python312 --trigger-http
```

## Platform Comparison

| Feature | Heroku | AWS | GCP |
|---------|--------|-----|-----|
| Ease of use | Easiest | Complex | Moderate |
| Cost | Higher per unit | Pay-as-you-go | Pay-as-you-go |
| Scale | Limited | Unlimited | Unlimited |
| Best for | MVPs, small apps | Enterprise | Data/ML workloads |

## Common Mistakes
- Using Heroku free tier for production (sleeps after inactivity)
- Not setting Cloud Run minimum instances, causing cold starts

## Key Takeaways
- Heroku is fastest to production but most expensive at scale
- Cloud Run: bring your container, get autoscaling
- Choose your cloud based on team expertise, not hype
7.4.3.intuition: Heroku is like a fully furnished apartment -- move in immediately but pay premium rent. AWS is like buying land and building from scratch -- maximum control but requires expertise. GCP Cloud Run is like a modern co-working space -- bring your laptop (container) and everything else is handled for you.

exercises.title: Deploy to Heroku
exercises.description: Deploy a FastAPI application to Heroku with a PostgreSQL database. Create a Procfile, runtime.txt, and configure environment variables. Verify the app works with the managed database.
exercises.hint: Use gunicorn with uvicorn workers in the Procfile: web: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT

exercises.title: Deploy to Cloud Run
exercises.description: Containerize a Python application and deploy it to GCP Cloud Run. Configure environment variables, set up a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance, and connect them.
exercises.hint: Use the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar in Cloud Run to connect to Cloud SQL securely.

exercises.title: Cost Comparison
exercises.description: For a small application serving 100k requests/month with a PostgreSQL database, calculate the monthly cost on Heroku, AWS (EC2+RDS), and GCP (Cloud Run+Cloud SQL). Document trade-offs.
exercises.hint: Check the pricing calculators for each platform. Consider compute, database, bandwidth, and storage costs.

problems.problem: Heroku app crashes with H10 error
problems.cause: The application failed to bind to the PORT environment variable that Heroku assigns dynamically.
problems.solution: Use `--port $PORT` in your Procfile. Never hardcode a port number on Heroku.
symptoms: Web process failed to bind to $PORT

problems.problem: Cloud Run cold starts are too slow
problems.cause: Cloud Run scales to zero by default. The first request after idle time must start a new container.
problems.solution: Set minimum instances to 1: `gcloud run services update myapp --min-instances 1`. Optimize your Docker image size and application startup time.

7.5.1.body:
# WSGI vs ASGI: Gunicorn and Uvicorn

WSGI and ASGI are interfaces between your Python web application and the web server. Understanding them is crucial for choosing the right application server.

## WSGI - Synchronous

```python
def application(environ, start_response):
    start_response('200 OK', [('Content-Type', 'text/plain')])
    return [b'Hello, World!']
```

```bash
# Gunicorn (standard WSGI server)
gunicorn app:create_app() \
  --workers 4 \
  --threads 2 \
  --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 \
  --timeout 30 \
  --access-logfile -
# Worker count rule: (2 * CPU cores) + 1
```

## ASGI - Asynchronous

```python
async def application(scope, receive, send):
    if scope['type'] == 'http':
        await send({'type': 'http.response.start', 'status': 200,
                    'headers': [[b'content-type', b'text/plain']]})
        await send({'type': 'http.response.body', 'body': b'Hello, async!'})
```

```bash
# Development
uvicorn app.main:app --reload

# Production: Gunicorn with Uvicorn workers
gunicorn app.main:app \
  -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker \
  --workers 4 \
  --bind 0.0.0.0:8000
```

## When to Use Which

| Feature | WSGI (Gunicorn) | ASGI (Uvicorn) |
|---------|-----------------|----------------|
| Frameworks | Flask, Django | FastAPI, Starlette |
| Async/WebSockets | No | Yes |
| Use case | Traditional APIs | Real-time, async APIs |

## Common Mistakes
- Using `uvicorn --reload` in production
- Setting too many workers, exhausting server memory
- Not using Gunicorn as process manager for Uvicorn in production

## Key Takeaways
- Use Gunicorn + Uvicorn workers for production ASGI apps
- Workers = (2 * CPU cores) + 1 is a good starting point
- ASGI supports WebSockets; WSGI does not
7.5.1.intuition: WSGI is like a restaurant where each waiter (worker) handles one table at a time -- simple and reliable. ASGI is like a waiter who can take drink orders from table 1, check on table 2, and serve food to table 3 without waiting idle. For a busy restaurant with lots of quick orders (API calls), async waiters handle more tables.

exercises.title: Benchmark WSGI vs ASGI
exercises.description: Create identical endpoints in Flask (WSGI) and FastAPI (ASGI) that simulate an I/O-bound operation (sleep for 100ms). Benchmark both with wrk or ab, comparing throughput with 4 workers.
exercises.hint: Use time.sleep for Flask and asyncio.sleep for FastAPI. Run wrk -t4 -c100 -d10s.

exercises.title: Production Gunicorn Config
exercises.description: Create a gunicorn.conf.py file with production settings: calculated worker count, Uvicorn worker class, graceful timeout, access logging, and pre-fork hooks for initialization.
exercises.hint: Use multiprocessing.cpu_count() for dynamic worker calculation.

problems.problem: Gunicorn workers keep getting killed
problems.cause: Workers exceed the timeout (default 30s) because of long-running requests, or the server runs out of memory.
problems.solution: Increase timeout for long operations: `--timeout 120`. Reduce workers if memory is the issue. For truly long tasks, use a background worker (Celery).
symptoms: Worker with pid XXXX was terminated due to signal 9

problems.problem: Uvicorn reload doesn't pick up changes
problems.cause: The reload watcher doesn't detect changes in certain file types or mounted volumes (Docker).
problems.solution: Use `--reload-dir src` to specify which directory to watch. In Docker, ensure the volume mount is working and use `--reload-delay 0.5`.
symptoms: Have to manually restart the server

7.5.2.intuition: Nginx is like a receptionist at a busy office. They greet visitors (handle connections), hand out brochures (static files) directly without bothering the staff, and route complex requests to the right employee (application server). The receptionist also manages the queue so no single employee gets overwhelmed (load balancing).

exercises.title: Set Up Nginx Reverse Proxy
exercises.description: Configure Nginx as a reverse proxy for a Gunicorn-served Python application. Serve static files directly from Nginx, set proper proxy headers, and configure access logging.
exercises.hint: Use proxy_pass to Gunicorn's port. Use the alias directive for static files.

exercises.title: Load Balancer Configuration
exercises.description: Set up Nginx to load balance across 3 Gunicorn instances on different ports. Implement health checks and configure a backup server. Test failover by stopping one instance.
exercises.hint: Use the upstream block with least_conn and proxy_next_upstream for failover.

exercises.title: Rate Limiting
exercises.description: Configure Nginx rate limiting to allow 10 requests per second per IP for the API and 100 requests per second for static assets. Return a custom 429 response.
exercises.hint: Use limit_req_zone and limit_req directives with different zones for different locations.

problems.problem: 502 Bad Gateway error
problems.cause: Nginx cannot connect to the upstream application server (Gunicorn/Uvicorn isn't running or is on a different port).
problems.solution: Verify the application server is running: `systemctl status myapp`. Check that the proxy_pass port matches the application server's bind port. Check Nginx error logs: `tail /var/log/nginx/error.log`.
symptoms: connect() failed (111: Connection refused) in error.log

problems.problem: File uploads fail with 413 error
problems.cause: Nginx's default `client_max_body_size` is 1MB.
problems.solution: Add `client_max_body_size 10M;` (or your desired limit) in the server or location block.

problems.problem: WebSocket connections fail through Nginx
problems.cause: Nginx doesn't forward WebSocket upgrade headers by default.

7.5.3.intuition: SSL termination at Nginx is like having a security guard at the building entrance who handles all the ID checks and pat-downs. Once visitors pass through security, they can walk freely inside the building (internal network). The employees (your app) don't need to worry about security checks -- that's all handled at the door.

exercises.title: SSL Setup with Certbot
exercises.description: Set up HTTPS for a domain using Certbot with Nginx. Configure HTTP to HTTPS redirect, enable HTTP/2, add security headers (HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options), and verify with SSL Labs.
exercises.hint: Run certbot --nginx and then manually enhance the config with security headers and HTTP/2.

exercises.title: Self-Signed Certificate for Development
exercises.description: Create a self-signed SSL certificate for local development and configure Nginx to use it. Document why self-signed certs should never be used in production.
exercises.hint: Use openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 to generate the cert and key.

problems.problem: Infinite redirect loop after enabling HTTPS
problems.cause: The application is checking the protocol and redirecting to HTTPS, but Nginx is proxying as HTTP internally. The app doesn't know it's behind HTTPS.
problems.solution: Pass the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header from Nginx: `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;`. Configure your framework to trust this header (e.g., Django's SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER).

problems.problem: Certificate expired and site shows security warning
problems.cause: Let's Encrypt certificates expire every 90 days and auto-renewal failed.
problems.solution: Run `sudo certbot renew` manually. Check renewal timer: `sudo systemctl status certbot.timer`. Test with `sudo certbot renew --dry-run`.

problems.problem: Mixed content warnings after HTTPS migration
problems.cause: Some resources (images, scripts, CSS) are still loaded via HTTP.
problems.solution: Update all internal URLs to use HTTPS or protocol-relative URLs (//). Use Content-Security-Policy headers to enforce HTTPS. Check browser console for specific mixed content errors.

further_reading.title: SSL Configuration Generator
further_reading.note: Mozilla's tool to generate secure SSL configurations for Nginx, Apache, etc.

further_reading.title: SSL Labs Server Test
further_reading.note: Free online tool to test and grade your HTTPS configuration

7.6.1.intuition: Terraform is like a blueprint for a building. You describe what rooms (resources) you want, and Terraform is the contractor who builds exactly that. If you change the blueprint (add a room), the contractor only builds the new room without tearing down the existing ones. The state file is the contractor's notebook tracking what has been built so far.

exercises.title: Provision a Web Stack
exercises.description: Write Terraform configuration to create an EC2 instance, an RDS PostgreSQL database, and an S3 bucket. Use variables for instance type, region, and database credentials. Output the EC2 public IP and RDS endpoint.
exercises.hint: Use aws_instance, aws_db_instance, and aws_s3_bucket resources. Reference variables with var.name.

exercises.title: Remote State Setup
exercises.description: Configure Terraform to use an S3 backend for remote state storage with DynamoDB for state locking. Create the S3 bucket and DynamoDB table first, then migrate local state to remote.
exercises.hint: Create the S3 bucket and DynamoDB table manually or with a separate Terraform config. Use terraform init -migrate-state.

exercises.title: Terraform Modules
exercises.description: Refactor a monolithic Terraform configuration into reusable modules: one for networking (VPC, subnets), one for compute (EC2), and one for database (RDS).
exercises.hint: Create a modules/ directory with subdirectories. Use module blocks in root main.tf to call them.

problems.problem: Terraform state is out of sync with actual infrastructure
problems.cause: Someone modified resources manually through the AWS console.
problems.solution: Run `terraform plan` to see drift. Use `terraform import` to bring manually created resources under Terraform management. Use `terraform refresh` to update state from actual infrastructure.
symptoms: terraform plan shows changes you didn't make

problems.problem: State lock error prevents terraform apply
problems.cause: A previous terraform operation crashed without releasing the DynamoDB lock.
problems.solution: Use `terraform force-unlock <LOCK_ID>` with the lock ID from the error message. Verify no other operation is genuinely running first.

further_reading.title: Terraform Up & Running (Yevgeniy Brikman)
further_reading.note: The best book for learning Terraform from basics to advanced patterns

7.6.2.body:
# Kubernetes Introduction: Pods, Services, Deployments

Kubernetes (K8s) orchestrates containerized applications at scale, handling deployment, scaling, and self-healing automatically.

## Core Concepts

- **Pod**: Smallest unit. One or more containers sharing network/storage
- **Service**: Stable endpoint routing traffic to pods
- **Deployment**: Declares desired state and manages rollouts

## Deployment Manifest

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: myapp }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: myapp }
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: myapp:1.2.0
          ports: [{ containerPort: 8000 }]
          resources:
            requests: { memory: "128Mi", cpu: "250m" }
            limits: { memory: "256Mi", cpu: "500m" }
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /health, port: 8000 }
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: myapp-service
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector: { app: myapp }
  ports: [{ port: 80, targetPort: 8000 }]
```

## Essential kubectl Commands

```bash
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl get pods
kubectl logs myapp-abc123 -f
kubectl exec -it myapp-abc123 -- bash
kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas=5
kubectl rollout undo deployment myapp  # Rollback
```

## Common Mistakes
- Not setting resource requests/limits
- Missing readiness probes, sending traffic to unready pods
- Using `latest` image tag, making rollbacks impossible

## Key Takeaways
- Always set resource limits and readiness probes
- Use specific image tags for reproducible deployments
- K8s self-heals: crashed pods are automatically restarted
7.6.2.intuition: Kubernetes is like a fleet manager for delivery trucks (containers). You tell the manager 'I need 3 trucks running route A.' If one breaks down, the manager automatically dispatches a replacement. If demand increases, you say 'make it 5 trucks' and two more are deployed. The Service is like a dispatch phone number that always reaches an available truck.

exercises.title: Deploy to Kubernetes
exercises.description: Write Kubernetes manifests to deploy a Python web application with 3 replicas, a Service exposing port 80, resource limits, readiness probes, and environment variables from a Secret. Apply to a local cluster (minikube or kind).
exercises.hint: Create the Secret first with kubectl create secret. Use readinessProbe with httpGet to your health endpoint.

exercises.title: Rolling Update and Rollback
exercises.description: Deploy version 1 of an application, then update to version 2 using kubectl set image. Monitor the rollout, then rollback to version 1. Document the process and verify zero downtime.
exercises.hint: Use kubectl rollout status, kubectl rollout history, and kubectl rollout undo.

exercises.title: ConfigMap and Secrets
exercises.description: Create a ConfigMap for non-sensitive configuration (app settings, feature flags) and a Secret for sensitive data (database URL, API keys). Mount them as environment variables in your deployment.
exercises.hint: Use kubectl create configmap and kubectl create secret generic. Reference with envFrom in the pod spec.

problems.problem: Pod stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
problems.cause: The container keeps crashing on startup. Common causes: missing environment variables, failed database connection, application error.
problems.solution: Check logs: `kubectl logs <pod-name> --previous` to see the crash output. Check events: `kubectl describe pod <pod-name>`. Fix the application error or missing configuration.

problems.problem: Pod stuck in Pending state
problems.cause: Not enough resources (CPU/memory) available on any node to schedule the pod.
problems.solution: Check events: `kubectl describe pod <pod-name>`. Reduce resource requests, add more nodes, or remove other workloads. Check for node taints or affinity rules.

problems.problem: Service not routing traffic to pods
problems.cause: Label selector mismatch between the Service and the Deployment pods.
problems.solution: Verify labels match: `kubectl get pods --show-labels` and compare with `kubectl describe service myapp-service`. The Service selector must match the pod template labels exactly.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes the Hard Way
further_reading.note: Deep learning exercise -- set up K8s from scratch to understand every component

7.6.3.body:
# Docker Swarm vs Kubernetes: Choosing an Orchestrator

Both orchestrate containers but differ in complexity, features, and use cases.

## Docker Swarm

```bash
docker swarm init
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml myapp
docker service scale myapp_web=5
docker service ls
```

```yaml
services:
  web:
    image: myapp:1.2.0
    deploy:
      replicas: 3
      update_config:
        parallelism: 1
        delay: 10s
      restart_policy:
        condition: on-failure
      resources:
        limits: { cpus: '0.5', memory: 256M }
    ports: ["8000:8000"]
    networks: [app-net]

networks:
  app-net:
    driver: overlay
```

## Comparison

| Feature | Docker Swarm | Kubernetes |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| Setup | Minutes | Hours/days |
| Learning curve | Low | Steep |
| Auto-scaling | Basic | Advanced (HPA) |
| Ecosystem | Small | Massive |
| Best for | Small teams, <10 services | Large teams, complex apps |

## Decision Framework

```python
if team_size < 5 and services < 10:
    use = "Docker Swarm"
elif need_multi_cloud or enterprise_scale:
    use = "Kubernetes"
elif want_managed_service:
    use = "ECS/Cloud Run"
```

## Common Mistakes
- Choosing K8s for a 3-service app (over-engineering)
- Choosing Swarm when you'll need advanced autoscaling soon
- Not considering managed services (ECS, Cloud Run)

## Key Takeaways
- Swarm is simpler but has declining community adoption
- K8s is the industry standard but operationally complex
- For small-to-medium apps, managed container services are often best
7.6.3.intuition: Choosing between Swarm and Kubernetes is like choosing between a reliable minivan and a semi truck. The minivan (Swarm) is easy to drive, great for small loads, and you can start using it immediately. The semi truck (Kubernetes) can haul massive cargo, has advanced GPS and fleet management, but requires a commercial license and training to operate. Don't rent a semi to move a couch.

exercises.title: Deploy with Docker Swarm
exercises.description: Initialize a Docker Swarm cluster (single node for learning), deploy a multi-service application using docker stack deploy with a compose file. Scale a service, perform a rolling update, and observe the behavior.
exercises.hint: Use docker swarm init, docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml myapp, and docker service scale.

exercises.title: Migration Assessment
exercises.description: Given a docker-compose.yml with 5 services, evaluate what it would take to migrate to Kubernetes. Write the equivalent Kubernetes manifests (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps) and document the added complexity vs. benefits.
exercises.hint: Create a K8s manifest for each service. Note what Docker Compose handles implicitly (networking, DNS) that K8s requires explicit configuration for.

exercises.title: Orchestrator Decision Matrix
exercises.description: For three scenarios (startup MVP with 2 developers, mid-size SaaS with 20 microservices, enterprise multi-region deployment), evaluate and recommend the best orchestration approach with justification.
exercises.hint: Consider team size, service count, scaling needs, budget, and available expertise for each scenario.

problems.problem: Docker Swarm service stuck at 0/N replicas
problems.cause: The image cannot be pulled, or resource constraints prevent scheduling.
problems.solution: Check service logs: `docker service ps myapp_web --no-trunc` to see error messages. Verify the image exists and is accessible. Check node resources with `docker node ls`.
symptoms: No container running for the service

problems.problem: Cannot decide between orchestrators
problems.cause: Analysis paralysis from comparing features without considering actual needs.
problems.solution: Start with the simplest option that meets your current needs. For most teams: use Docker Compose for development, a managed service (ECS/Cloud Run) for production, and only adopt Kubernetes when you genuinely need its features (auto-scaling, service mesh, multi-cluster).
symptoms: Weeks spent evaluating tools instead of shipping
Over-engineered infrastructure for a simple app

further_reading.title: You Don't Need Kubernetes
further_reading.note: A pragmatic perspective on when simpler alternatives are better
